title: Almond tuiles
subtitle: Thin, translucent almond wafers curved over a rolling pin
excerpt: Lacy almond biscuits spread paper-thin, baked until golden, and curved over a rolling pin while still hot and pliable. Fragile and elegant, they are made to break at the table.
sourceNotes: Tuiles (French for roof tiles, describing the curved shape) appear in French patisserie literature from the eighteenth century. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), Project Gutenberg, gives the almond tuile as a standard garnish for desserts and ice creams. The shaping technique of curving warm biscuits over a rolling pin is consistent across French patisserie tradition.

content.text: are one of the more technically demanding small biscuits in French patisserie, not because the batter is complex (it takes five minutes to mix) but because the shaping window is narrow. Each biscuit must come off the tray while still warm, be draped over a rolling pin, and be allowed to cool in that curved position. Once cold, it is rigid and fragile. If you miss the window (about 20 to 30 seconds per biscuit), it cracks when you try to curve it.

Bake three or four at a time rather than a full tray. This gives enough time to shape one while the next still has time to be soft. The batter is very forgiving; it can rest in the fridge for several days and the flavour improves.

Mix {{caster-sugar}}, {{plain-flour}}, and {{blanched-almonds}} together. Add {{egg-whites}}, melted {{unsalted-butter}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} and stir to a loose, wet batter. Refrigerate for at least 30 minutes; this makes spreading easier and produces a crisper result.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan (200°C conventional). Line a baking tray with baking paper. Drop teaspoons of batter well apart on the tray, no more than four per batch. Spread each one to a thin circle about 8 cm across with the back of a spoon. Bake for 6 to 8 minutes until the edges are golden-brown and the centres are lightly coloured. Remove from the oven.

Working immediately, lift each biscuit from the tray with a palette knife and drape it over a rolling pin. It will conform to the curve as it cools; this takes about 30 seconds. If a biscuit cools before you can shape it and becomes brittle, return the whole tray to the oven for 30 to 45 seconds to soften it again.
Tuiles absorb moisture from the air quickly in humid conditions. Serve within two hours of shaping or store in an airtight tin with a sachet of silica gel.
Flat tuiles (not curved) are easier and work well as a garnish for panna cotta or ice cream. Simply leave them on the cooling rack without shaping.
Orange zest in the batter, replacing the vanilla, is the classic French version.
